name: Ishtar's Descent
shortDescription: Ishtar's Descent is a Sumerian myth where the goddess Inanna (Ishtar in Akkadian) travels to the underworld, Kur, and faces trials and tribulations, ultimately leading to her death and resurrection. It explores themes of power, death, sacrifice, and renewal.
description: Ishtar's Descent is a prominent Sumerian myth detailing the journey of the goddess Inanna (Ishtar), queen of heaven and earth, into the underworld, known as Kur. Motivated by a desire to witness the funeral rites of Gugalanna, the deceased husband of Ereshkigal, the queen of the underworld, Inanna prepares meticulously for her journey, adorning herself in regal attire and jewelry. She instructs her minister, Ninshubur, to seek aid from the gods Enlil, Nanna, and Enki if she does not return after a certain period. As Inanna passes through the seven gates of the underworld, she is forced to remove an item of clothing or jewelry at each gate, symbolizing the stripping away of her power and status. Upon reaching Ereshkigal, she is judged, killed, and hung on a hook. The world above suffers in her absence; fertility ceases, and procreation stops. Ninshubur follows Inanna's instructions and pleads with the gods. Enki, the god of wisdom, creates two sexless beings (gala-tura and kur-jara) to appease Ereshkigal and retrieve Inanna's body. They empathize with Ereshkigal's pain and are rewarded with Inanna's corpse. However, a substitute is required for Inanna to leave the underworld. Demons pursue her, and she eventually designates her husband, Dumuzi, as her replacement, angered by his lack of mourning. Dumuzi's sister, Geshtinanna, eventually agrees to share his time in the underworld, resulting in a cyclical pattern of death and rebirth, possibly connected to the changing seasons. The myth explores themes of power, death, the cyclical nature of life, the balance between the upper and lower worlds, and the complex relationship between deities. It also highlights the importance of mourning and sacrifice in maintaining cosmic order.

sources.title: Inanna, Queen of Heaven and Earth
sources.author: Diane Wolkstein and Samuel Noah Kramer

sources.title: The Descent of Ishtar to the Nether World
sources.author: Translated by E.A. Speiser
